findings.summary: Two parallel performance monitoring modules with overlapping but distinct APIs coexist in infrastructure/core/
evidence: infrastructure/core/performance.py defines PerformanceMetrics, ResourceUsage, PerformanceMonitor
infrastructure/core/performance_monitor.py defines ProfilingMetrics, CodeProfiler with overlapping profiling purpose
infrastructure/core/__init__.py imports both, with a redundant alias: 'monitor_performance as monitor_performance_decorator' indicating confusion about which module owns the concept
Both modules are tested independently (test_performance.py and test_performance_monitor.py), confirming duplication is not accidental re-export
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into a single performance module; PerformanceMonitor (pipeline-level) and CodeProfiler (microbenchmark) can be in separate submodules if the distinction is intentional, but they should not sit flat in core/ as peers with overlapping names

findings.summary: Several optional dependency groups (dashboard, steganography, dotenv) are silently absent at runtime, creating inconsistent feature availability
evidence: pyproject.toml defines 'dashboard', 'steganography', 'dotenv', 'llm' as optional dependency-groups — not installed by default
infrastructure/__init__.py wraps all imports in a bare try/except ImportError, emitting only ImportWarning — callers cannot distinguish 'module missing' from 'module broken'
The steganography block catches ImportError and warns, but production pipeline code may assume those symbols exist without checking, causing AttributeError at runtime
findings.suggestion: Use explicit feature-flag checks (e.g., HAS_STEGANOGRAPHY = False at module level, set to True on successful import) rather than swallowing ImportError and allowing partial symbol availability; document which pipeline stages require which optional groups

findings.summary: pytest is listed as a runtime dependency rather than a dev-only dependency in the default required packages check
evidence: check_dependencies() default required_packages list includes 'pytest' alongside 'numpy', 'matplotlib', 'requests'

pytest is a test tool, not a runtime concern — importing it at application startup pollutes the runtime dependency surface and misleads downstream users who run check_dependencies() to assess production readiness
findings.suggestion: Remove pytest from the default required_packages list in check_dependencies(); it belongs only in the dev dependency group

findings.summary: Three files with 3+ importers each have zero test coverage, including LLM template modules used across the pipeline
evidence: holistic_context.testing.critical_untested lists infrastructure/llm/templates/manuscript.py (3 importers), infrastructure/llm/templates/base.py (3 importers), and projects/cognitive_case_diagrams/src/visualization/styles.py (3 importers) as having no tests
LLM template modules are complex string-generation paths where regressions are hard to catch without tests
The project mandates 90% coverage for project code but cognitive_case_diagrams/src/visualization/styles.py is uncovered
findings.suggestion: Add unit tests for manuscript.py and base.py covering at minimum the template rendering paths; add tests for styles.py verifying the returned style dictionaries have expected keys

findings.summary: The project's stated no-mocks policy is violated in test_logging.py, which uses unittest.mock.patch
evidence: Grep for MagicMock/unittest.mock/mocker.patch found tests/infra_tests/llm/test_logging.py as the only match
The file uses 'from unittest.mock import patch' at lines 375 and 398
CLAUDE.md and pyproject.toml both state 'Never use MagicMock, mocker.patch, unittest.mock' as an ABSOLUTE REQUIREMENT
findings.suggestion: Replace the patch() calls in test_logging.py with real log records and real LLM client stubs using pytest-httpserver or real configuration objects, consistent with every other test file in the suite

findings.summary: New test files (test_errors.py, test_health_check.py, test_publishing_core.py) test only structural contracts and happy paths, not failure branches
evidence: test_errors.py tests format() output strings but does not test what happens when format() is called with extra unexpected kwargs (which Python str.format silently ignores)

test_health_check.py asserts that unhealthy checks have an 'error' key, but only when status is 'unhealthy' — there is no test that forces a check to be unhealthy to verify the error key is populated correctly

test_publishing_core.py tests the happy path of create_publication_package with real files but does not test behaviour when output_dir is not writable or when metadata fields contain special characters
findings.suggestion: Add at least one failure-path test per module: force a hash mismatch in file_operations, inject a bad health check result, and pass a title with slashes/special chars to create_publication_package

findings.summary: ProjectLogger method docstrings restate the function name verbatim with no added information
evidence: Line 86: def debug(...): '"""Log debug message."""' — the docstring adds zero information beyond the name 'debug'
Lines 90, 93, 97, 101 repeat the exact same pattern: 'Log info message.', 'Log warning message.', 'Log error message.', 'Log critical message.'
Line 109: def header(...): '"""Log section header."""' — again, entirely restated from the method name
Line 113: def progress(...): '"""Log progress with percentage."""' — at least mentions 'with percentage', which is marginally useful, but the pattern is systematic
findings.suggestion: Either drop these one-line restate docstrings entirely (the method names are self-explanatory) or replace them with the one fact that is not obvious — e.g., what emoji prefix is used, or what log level is selected

findings.summary: install_missing_packages() carries a six-line docstring explaining uv internals that belong in a module-level comment, not per-function
evidence: install_missing_packages() docstring (lines 126-147) explains the overall uv philosophy ('uses uv add to properly manage dependencies through pyproject.toml, then runs uv sync') — this is already in the module docstring

The function body itself is straightforward: check for uv, run uv add, run uv sync; the docstring's 'Attempts to install packages using uv package manager for speed and reliability' restates the function name
The module-level docstring (lines 1-33) already explains the uv integration strategy in detail, making the per-function repetition redundant
findings.suggestion: Trim the function docstring to Args/Returns only; keep uv integration rationale at the module level where it was already documented

findings.summary: get_project_logger() is a pure one-line wrapper over ProjectLogger() constructor with no added policy
evidence: get_project_logger() at line 147 contains exactly: 'return ProjectLogger(name, level)' — it forwards both arguments unchanged

setup_project_logging() at line 169 also wraps ProjectLogger via get_project_logger() with only an optional file setup side-effect, creating a three-layer call for what is effectively 'create a logger'
Both wrappers have multi-line docstrings with Example: blocks, amplifying the boilerplate surface
findings.suggestion: Remove get_project_logger() and have callers instantiate ProjectLogger directly; keep setup_project_logging() only if the file-handler setup is genuinely reused, but document it as the only entry point

findings.summary: log_function_call decorator emits entry and exit logs on every decorated function, consistent with AI-generated noisy logging pattern
evidence: log_function_call() decorator (lines 435-472) logs 'Calling: {func.__name__}' on entry and 'Completed: {func.__name__} ({duration}s)' on exit for every invocation
This produces redundant noise for short-lived functions and duplicates what log_operation() context manager already provides
The decorator is a classic AI-generated pattern: generic entry/exit tracing applied uniformly without considering per-function value
findings.suggestion: Restrict the decorator to functions where timing is genuinely diagnostic (e.g., expensive I/O); remove the entry log or make it DEBUG-level only to avoid noise at the default INFO level

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ contains 27 files (9,644 LOC) with mixed concerns: logging (4 files), performance (2 files), pipeline, security, credentials, menus, and progress
evidence: infrastructure/core/ has 27 production files — the packet reports file_count: 27 with total_loc: 9644
Logging alone spans 5 files: logging_constants, logging_formatters, logging_helpers, logging_progress, logging_utils — these form a subsystem that could be a core/logging/ subpackage
security.py (RateLimiter, SecurityHeaders, SecurityMonitor, SecurityValidator) has no coupling to the rest of core/ and 6 importers from steganography/llm — it belongs in a security/ subpackage
menu.py is a terminal UI concern completely unrelated to config, logging, or pipeline; it has near-zero coupling to other core files
findings.suggestion: Extract infrastructure/core/logging_*.py into infrastructure/core/logging/, performance*.py into infrastructure/core/profiling/, and security.py + credentials.py into infrastructure/security/ — this reduces the flat-directory overload and groups files by cohesion

findings.summary: validate_markdown_cli.py lives inside infrastructure/validation/ but re-exports functions and patches sys.path, blurring the CLI boundary from the library boundary
evidence: validate_markdown_cli.py inserts repo_root into sys.path at line 21 inside a library package — this is a CLI-level concern inside a library module
It defines helper re-exports (find_markdown_files) described as 'for testing', making it a hybrid CLI+library module

CLI entry points are also present in infrastructure/rendering/render_all_cli.py and infrastructure/publishing/publish_cli.py — the pattern is inconsistent: some CLIs live in their subdirectory, others are standalone scripts
findings.suggestion: Move all *_cli.py entry points to a top-level cli/ package or scripts/ directory; keep infrastructure/validation/ as a pure library with no sys.path manipulation or re-exports for test convenience

findings.summary: infrastructure/steganography/ is a domain-specific optional feature module placed as a peer of foundational infrastructure packages
evidence: infrastructure/__init__.py wraps steganography import separately from all other infrastructure imports, acknowledging it is optional and unlike the others
Steganography has low fan-in (84 snake_case identifiers across a small module) and is completely optional per pyproject.toml's 'steganography' dependency group
It is structurally a plugin/extension, not foundational infrastructure — placing it under infrastructure/ gives it false equivalence with core/, rendering/, and validation/
findings.suggestion: Move infrastructure/steganography/ to plugins/steganography/ or projects/extensions/steganography/ to signal its optional, domain-specific nature and separate it from the foundational infrastructure layer

findings.summary: infrastructure/publishing/core.py is a pure re-export shim that only imports and re-exports from sibling modules with no logic of its own
evidence: infrastructure/publishing/core.py lines 24-48 consist entirely of 'from infrastructure.publishing.X import ... # noqa: F401' re-exports from four sibling modules
The only function defined in core.py itself is create_publication_package(), making it a mixed shim+logic file
infrastructure/publishing/__init__.py then re-exports from core.py, creating a two-hop import chain for most publishing symbols: caller -> __init__ -> core -> citations/metadata/models/platforms
findings.suggestion: Either consolidate the re-exports into __init__.py directly (eliminating core.py as a shim layer) or move create_publication_package() into a more specific module; do not let core.py serve as both a re-export facade and a logic module
